mnemonics.title: Vigenère Encryption
mnemonics.content: Classic Vigenère: Add Plain to Key (mod 26). Remember: C = (P + K) mod 26. For decryption, flip the sign: P = (C - K) mod 26.

mnemonics.title: IC Values
mnemonics.content: IC of 065 for English plain, 0385 for random rain (uniform distribution). Think: 6.5% for English, ~3.85% for random.

mnemonics.title: Kasiski Method
mnemonics.content: Kasiski: Keep Looking For Repetitions - find Repeated sequences, measure distances, find GCD to crack the key.

mnemonics.title: Perfect Secrecy Requirements
mnemonics.content: OTP Requirements: One-time, Purely random, Length = message, Perfect secrecy.

acronyms.usage: Measures letter frequency patterns to distinguish monoalphabetic from polyalphabetic ciphers. Higher IC (~0.065) indicates structured language, lower (~0.0385) indicates polyalphabetic or random text.
Method to determine key length by finding repeated sequences and analyzing distances between them in the ciphertext.
A perfectly secure polyalphabetic cipher where key equals message length, is random, and used only once.
Statistical test using IC to estimate the probable key length of a polyalphabetic cipher: k = (0.0265n)/(0.065IC - 0.0265n + 0.0265)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 26x26 grid where each row is the alphabet shifted one position left from the row above. Row 0 = A to Z, Row 1 = B to Z+A, etc. To encrypt: find row = key letter, column = plaintext letter, intersection = ciphertext.

In monoalphabetic ciphers, frequency bars show distinct peaks (E, T, A high). In polyalphabetic, these peaks flatten out - imagine smearing the bars until they become nearly equal height, hiding the original frequencies.
Picture the key as a conveyor belt cycling over the plaintext: KEYKEYKEYKEY... above ATTACKATDAWN. Each letter gets shifted by the letter below it in the repeating pattern.
Think of IC as a measure of 'bumpiness' in letter distribution. English has bumpy terrain (high IC), random text is flat (low IC). Polyalphabetic flattens the terrain, reducing IC.

analogies.analogy: Monoalphabetic is like speaking one language throughout (easy to understand patterns). Polyalphabetic is like mixing multiple languages mid-sentence - even if you know each language, you can't tell when each switches, making it harder to decode.

The key period is like a combination lock with multiple wheels. Each wheel (key letter) is used in sequence. With more wheels (longer key), more combinations exist and it's harder to guess the pattern.

Imagine counting colors in a painting. If all red objects are always painted blue (monoalphabetic), you can easily figure out the pattern. If red can be blue, green, or yellow depending on position (polyalphabetic), counting becomes useless.

The one-time pad is like a one-time use decoder ring that burns after reading. Each message gets a unique completely random key, used once, then destroyed - making past intercepts useless for future messages.
